coredata.dc:title: Corrosion inhibition of high-nitrogen-doped CDs for copper in 3wt% NaCl solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tingting
Zhang, Daquan
Wu, Panpan
Gao, Lixin
coredata.dc:description:
Background Carbon dots (CDs) can be designed as an effective assembly unit to construct the intelligent corrosion inhibition system. Compared to purified CDs, the doped CDs have more advantageous anti-corrosion performance. However, the reported nitrogen-doped CDs usually have a low N/C atomic ratio. The increase of the N/C ratio has an important significance in improving the properties of nitrogen-doped CDs for corrosion protection.
Methods The high-nitrogen-doped CDs (N-doped CDs) are prepared from melamine and fructose via solvothermal treatment. The corrosion inhibition effect of the N-doped CDs for copper in 3wt% NaCl solution is studied by electrochemical measurements, surface characterizations and fluorescence spectrum analyzer. It demonstrates that the N-doped CDs can protect the copper in 3wt% NaCl solution effectively.
Significant findings The nitrogen content of the N-doped CDs is improved to 23.95%.The optimal inhibition efficiencies reach 96.1% at 200 mg⋅L−1 of the N-doped CDs. The N-doped CDs can form a compact protective film on copper surface via adsorption and chelation with cupric ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper
Quantum dots
Electrochemical measurement
Chlorination corrosion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the N-doped CDs preparation.

Characterization of the N-doped CDs: (a) UV–VIS absorption spectrum; (b) FL emission spectrum (Inset: pictures of the N-doped CDs under 365 nm UV light); (c) TEM images (Inset: element composition of the N-doped CDs from elemental analyzer); (d) FT-IR spectrum.

Polarization curves (a), electrochemical impedance spectra of Nyquist plots (b) and Bode plots (c) for copper electrode after 1h immersed in 3wt% NaCl solution containing the N-doped CDs. (25°C); the equivalent circuit model for EIS in 3wt% NaCl solution without (d) and with N-doped CDs (e).
SEM images (a, b) and contact angle appearance(c, d) of the copper after 24 h immersed in 3wt% NaCl solution without and with 200mg L-1 N-doped CDs.
Schematic illustration of inhibition mechanism on copper surface for the high-N-doped CDs in solution.
Polarization parameters of copper in in 3wt% NaCl solution with different concentrations of N-doped CDs.
The results of electrochemical parameters fitted in EIS for copper in 3wt% NaCl solution with different concentration of N-doped CDs.

head.ce:title: Corrosion inhibition of high-nitrogen-doped CDs for copper in 3wt% NaCl solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power, Shanghai University of Electric Power, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bShanghai Engineering Research Centre of Energy-Saving in Heat Exchange Systems, Shanghai 200090, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power, Shanghai University of Electric Power, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) can be designed as an effective assembly unit to construct the intelligent corrosion inhibition system. Compared to purified CDs, the doped CDs have more advantageous anti-corrosion performance. However, the reported nitrogen-doped CDs usually have a low N/C atomic ratio. The increase of the N/C ratio has an important significance in improving the properties of nitrogen-doped CDs for corrosion protection.

The high-nitrogen-doped CDs (N-doped CDs) are prepared from melamine and fructose via solvothermal treatment. The corrosion inhibition effect of the N-doped CDs for copper in 3wt% NaCl solution is studied by electrochemical measurements, surface characterizations and fluorescence spectrum analyzer. It demonstrates that the N-doped CDs can protect the copper in 3wt% NaCl solution effectively.

The nitrogen content of the N-doped CDs is improved to 23.95%.The optimal inhibition efficiencies reach 96.1% at 200 mg⋅L−1 of the N-doped CDs. The N-doped CDs can form a compact protective film on copper surface via adsorption and chelation with cupric ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are seen as new zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with the advantages of good biocompatibility, large specific surface area, and easy functionalization [1,2]. They have been widely applied in different fields, such as bioimaging, catalysts, sensors and photovoltaic devices [3,4]. Recently, CDs have attracted extensive attention in anti-corrosion field due to their water solubility and eco-friendly [5]. A large number of CDs derived from citric acid [6,7], ammonium citrate [8,9], urea [10,11], histidine [12,13], and thiourea [14,15] are investigated as corrosion inhibitors. They can be designed as an effective assembly unit to construct the intelligent corrosion inhibition system

It is well known that most organic corrosion inhibitors have heteroatoms (N, O, P and S, etc.) in their molecular structure [16]. Similarly, the heteroatoms doped in CDs can serve as active sites for adsorption due to their rich electrons [17]. Hence, the doped CDs can be seen as advantageous corrosion inhibitors compared to the purified CDs [18]. Nitrogen-doped CDs have been reported to exhibit the higher anti-corrosive activity. Cui et al. [19] synthesized the nitrogen-doped CDs (8.1%N) from antibiotic amino salicylic acid (ASA). Their corrosion inhibition efficiency can reach 93%-96% for Q235 steel in acid solution. Ye et al. [20] reported that the nitrogen-doped CDs using ammonium citrate as precursors had 90% inhibition efficiency on Q235 steel corrosion in the same acid solution. The nitrogen-doped carbon dots were reported to have a well inhibition effect on steel corrosion in the pickling process [21]. Luo et al. [22] discussed the reaction conditions on the inhibition efficiency of the nitrogen-doped CDs. They found that the nitrogen-doped CDs had the best performance at reaction temperature of 180 °C and the reaction time of 1 h. The best inhibition efficiency reached 90% for steel corrosion in 1 M HCl solution. Xu et al. [23] presents interesting new achievements relative to steel protection with the use of N-CQDs as corrosion inhibitor in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. From the most reports, the nitrogen-doped CDs have a satisfactory anti-corrosion effect for the metal in acidic solution [24]. It all comes down to the fact that the doped N atom can improve the internal electronic structure and bring new electronic distribution states for CDs [25].

However, the reported nitrogen-doped CDs usually have a low N/C atomic ratio, and N content is below 15 wt% [26]. Therefore, increasing the N/C ratio attracts intensive interest in improving the properties of CDs for corrosion protection [27]. Seldom reports regard the development of superior CDs inhibitor in the neutral solution. Moreover, the adsorption and anti-corrosion mechanisms for CDs in NaCl solution are not specific [22].

In this paper, we have prepared the high-nitrogen-doped CDs (N-doped CDs) by the easy and environmental friendly solvothermal treatment with melamine as the nitrogen source. The N-doped CDs is characterized by the fluorescence spectrum, the elemental analyzer (EA) and the morphology analyzer. The corrosion inhibition of the N-doped CDs for copper in the neutral solution is investigated using the electrochemical measurements and the surface analyzer. The inhibition mechanisms of the N-doped CDs in 3wt% NaCl solution is discussed and described in detail.

Melamine (C3H6N6, AR) was obtained through Sino Pharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Pure copper was purchased from Sheng Xin Technology Co., Ltd. Ethylene glycol (HOCH2CH2OH) was obtained from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. Fructose (C6H12O6) was bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. All the reagents were used without further purification. The working electrode (WE) was commonly made from pure copper (99.99 %) and sealed with epoxy resin with an exposure area of 1 cm2. Before the experiment, the WE was polished via a more refined piece of sandpaper and rinsed with anhydrous alcohol (99.5%).

Fig. 1 displays the preparation process of the functionalized N-doped CDs. The 0.1 g of melamine and 0.2 g of fructose were dissolved in ethylene glycol (20 mL). The admixture was moved into polytetrafluoroethylene autoclaves and heated at 200°C for 12h. Subsequently, the reaction products were percolated with filters (0.2 μm) to obtain filtrate. Then, a filtrate was filtered by a dialysis bag, which can retain 3500 Da molecular weight particles for 24 h to detach the impurities. In the early stage of the dialysis process, water was refreshed frequently. Finally, the remaining products were dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 24 h to obtain the N-doped CDs.

The element composition and chemical bonding of N-doped CDs were analyzed by the EA element analyzer (Vario EL, Elementar, Germany) and FT-IR (AVATAR-FTIP-360, Thermo- Nicolet, American), respectively. Morphology and size of N-doped CDs were characterized by TEM (JEM2100F, JEOL, Japan) [28,29]. The copper metal immersed in 3wt% NaCl solution with and without N-doped CDs at 25 °C was examined by FE-SEM (SEM-JSM-7800 F, JEOL, Japan). The optical characteristics of N-doped CDs were measured by fluorescence spectra (RF-5601PC, RF Micro Devices, American) and UVs spectra (UV1800ENG240V, Shimadzu, Japan) [30].

The polarization curves and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of copper electrodes were measured by the electrochemical workstation (CHI604E) at room temperature. After the open circuit potential was stable, the EIS and polarization curves were measured successively. The amplitude of sinusoidal excitation signal was 5 mV. And the frequency range was from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz for EIS. The scan range of polarization curve was from -250 to +250 mV (vs. Ecorr) at 0.167mV/s sweep rate. The experimental results were analyzed and fitted by Zplot and CorrWare software. The chi-square and the fitted error values were below 10−3 and 15 %, respectively.

One of the apparent features of the N-doped CDs is that they have firm absorption peaks in the ultraviolet region. UV–VIS and photoluminescence spectra are analyzed to investigate the optical properties of N-doped CDs. The UV–VIS absorption spectrum (Fig. 2 a) presents a single absorption peak at 323 nm. Fig. 2b also shows that N-doped CDs have fluorescence emission at 454 nm. They show bright blue fluorescence (inset in Fig. 2b) when they are dispersed in deionized water under UV excitation (365 nm). The optical properties may originate from n→π* transition of C=N bond and trapping of excited energy from the surface on nitrogen atom [31].

Fig. 2c displays the TEM images of the N-doped CDs inhibitor. It presents a spherical structure with size of about 7 nm. It also shows the content (wt%) of elements in N-doped CDs. The content (wt%) of elements in the N-doped CDs is investigated by EA, which comprise 47.65% C, 5.28% H and 23.95% N and surplus element O. Compared to 7.5%N [32], 8.1%N [19], 10.17%N [33] and 13.8%N [34], the nitrogen content has increased significantly, obviously.

Fig. 2d shows the FT-IR spectrum of the N-doped CDs. The strong absorption peak at 3297cm−1 is the N-H stretching vibration. The characteristic absorption peak of 2921cm−1 is the C-H stretching vibration. The special absorption peak of 1639cm−1 is the C=O vibration. The peaks at 1085 cm−1 and at 1042 cm−1 are attributed to the C-N vibration. The N-doped CDs contain some new functional groups after the hydrothermal reaction.

The potentiodynamic polarization curves are illustrated in Fig. 3 a. It contains of different curves obtained for copper in 3wt% NaCl solution with different concentrations of the N-doped CDs after 1 h immersion. The electrochemical parameters are listed in Table 1 . Inhibition efficiency (ϕ i ) is computed by Eq. (1).
(1)
φ i
% =
i
c o r r
0
−
i
c o r r
i
c o r r
0
×
100

As displayed in Fig. 3a, the anodic and cathodic reactions are visibly retarded after the addition of the N-doped CDs. From the perspective of the anodic branching, the N-doped CDs can significantly inhibit the active dissolution of copper. At the higher potential, the obvious increase of anode current density is observed, owing to the fall of the adsorption film formed on the surface of copper. It suggests that the desorption of N-doped CDs from the copper surface exist at a high anodic potential.

The Nyquist plots and the Bode plots are presented in Fig. 3b, c, respectively. These curves are obtained for copper electrode in 3wt% NaCl solution with and without the N-doped CDs. The EIS curves consist of an obvious capacitance arc in a high-frequency area. The diameter of capacitive reactance arc increases distinctly with the increase of CDs concentration. It is owing to the formation of inhibitive film on the copper surface [35]. It can be seen in Fig. 3c that the Zf=0.01 Hz value of copper in 3wt% NaCl solution is lower than that with different concentrations of the N-doped CDs. It proves that the N-doped CDs have good corrosion inhibition to copper. The Bode-phase angle plots are also shown in Fig. 3c. The two peaks of phase angles represent two time constants of the electrochemical reaction [36]. The time constant at the middle-high frequency is linked to the reaction of adsorption film. And the time constant at the low frequency is attributed to the charge transfer process [37]. The equivalent circuits are shown in Fig. 3d, e under the condition of the 3wt% NaCl solution without and with N-doped CDs. In Fig. 3e, the Rs, Rf, Rct Qf, and Qdl represent the solution resistance, the film resistance, the charge transfer resistance, the film capacitance and the double-layer capacitance, respectively [38]. The fitted data are listed in Table 2 . The corrosion inhibition efficiency (ϕ j ) is calculated according to the Eq. (2):
(2)
φ j
% =
R p
−
R p 0
R p
×
100

R p 0
is equal to Rct in 3wt% NaCl solution. And Rp is sum of Rf and Rct in the solutions with different concentrations of the N-doped CDs. Evidently, the N-doped CDs of different concentration bring the variation of Rct value [39]. The ϕ j % value can evaluate the inhibition efficiency [40]. The max values of ϕ j % reaches 96.1% when the N-doped CDs concentration is 200 mg/L, which is higher than some novel corrosion inhibitors for copper in NaCl solution, such as: microcrystalline cellulose-niacin composite (83.4%) [41], peel extract (92%) [42], and 1-octyl-3-methyl -midazolium L-tartrate (86.3%) [43]. Apart from the high corrosion inhibition efficiency, the N-doped CDs can be used as effective assembly units to construct of intelligent corrosion inhibition systems. Owing to the fluorescence emission, it can be developed as corrosion inhibitor with the self-diagnostic function. The protection of the N-doped CDs for metal varies with the change of its spectral property.

Both the enlargement in the diameter of Nyquist capacitive arc and the amplification of phase angle confirm this argument. In general, the N-doped CDs form the protective film at the interface between copper and 3wt% NaCl solution [44].

The micromorphology of copper after 24 h immersion in 3wt% NaCl solution with and without of the N-doped CDs is shown in Fig. 4 . In Fig. 4a, the copper surface is corroded severely. There are many pits and corrosion products on the copper surface. Unlike the corrosive environments of acidic solutions, the majority of corrosion products can not be dissolved in 3wt% NaCl solution [45,46]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the N-doped CDs are enriched on the copper surface. The anti-corrosion adsorption film formed on the surface of copper blocks the attack of the erosion ions.

The contact angle of deionized water on the surface of copper after 24 h immersed in 3wt% NaCl solution without and with 200 mg/L N-doped CDs solution is also shown in Fig. 4c, d. After adding N-doped CDs, the surface contact angle of copper increases from 88° to 134°, indicating that the adsorption of N-doped CDs on the copper surface significantly improves the interfacial hydrophobicity. The hydrophobic protective film can effectively inhibit the diffusion of corrosive ions into the metal substrate, and inhibit the corrosion of copper.

Organic corrosion inhibitors have been widely used for metal protection [47,48]. Generally, organic inhibitors containing heteroatoms (such as N, S, O, and P) can form an anti-corrosion adsorption film on the metal surface by physical adsorption or chemisorption between the metal and the inhibitors [49,50], thereby slowing down the metal corrosion rate. It shows Cu2+ ions can easily coordinate with heteroatoms in hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino groups, etc. [51,52]. As shown in the polarization curves (Fig. 3a), the N-doped CDs retard the dissolution process of copper and oxygen reduction reaction. The high-N-doped CDs consist of many functional groups, such as –NH2, –COOH, and –OH. They can act as the coordination sites with cupric ions. A series of interaction occur between the Cu2+ and N-doped CDs [53]. The high-N-doped CDs can strongly adsorb on the copper surface and enhance protective effect. From the SEM images (Fig. 4b), a number of the nanoparticles derivate from N-doped CDs are aggregated. The inhibition mechanism is schematically shown in Fig. 5 . The N-doped CDs adsorb on the copper surface. Some of them aggregate on the copper surface to form larger nanoparticles. When the dissolution of Cu2+ ions into 3wt% NaCl solution, the Cu2+ ions chelate with the N-doped CDs owing to the multi-coordination sites of the N-doped CDs. The coordination complex re-deposits on the surface of copper. The network of anti-corrosion film is formed through multiway connections between different aggregates and complexes. The protective film isolates direct contact between the corrosive medium and the copper surface, and supresses the corrosion reaction of copper.

Generally, the melamine is rich in nitrogen element (66.7 wt%). The high-N-doped CDs are obtained from melamine and fructose via the hydrothermal process. The N atom can provide a lone pair of electrons to form a coordinate bond to copper atom. The N-doped CDs have functional groups to provide the active site for adsorption. The chemical adsorption plays an important role to form the inhibitive film on the copper surface. The N-doped CDs can spontaneously adsorb on the copper surface via the interaction between its functional groups and copper substrate. On the other hand, when Cu2+ ions and N-doped CDs co-exist in the solution, the dissolved Cu2+ ions can also coordinate with the N-doped CDs in 3wt% NaCl solution. And the formed chelates can re-deposit onto the copper surface. This improves the compactness of the protective film, thereby slowing down copper corrosion significantly. The N-doped CDs show good protection for copper in the 3wt% NaCl solution. It will be an ideal candidate for the assembly unit to construct the intelligent corrosion inhibition system.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The N-doped CDs were successfully prepared by simple solvothermal reaction. The granule size of the N-doped CDs is about 7 nm.
The protective film of the N-doped CDs film on copper surface shows a rough morphology, accompanied by appearance of lots of nanoparticles.
The inhibition efficiency of the N-doped CDs can reach 96.1% for copper in 3wt% NaCl solution.
The coordination between Cu2+ ions and the N-doped CDs can effectively improve the compactness of the protective film.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Our work was supported by NSFC project (Grant No 52071198, 52001194). We thank the grant from the Science & Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (18DZ2204400, 19DZ2271100) very much.
